content:
First, we rephrase the condition “no two dominoes are side‐adjacent” in graph‐theoretic language.  Think of the $1000\times1000$ board as the grid‐graph $G$ whose vertices are the squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares.  A domino is then simply an edge of $G$, and the rule that no two dominoes be side‐adjacent means that the set of chosen edges is an *induced* matching in $G$:  no two chosen edges share a vertex or are joined by another edge of $G$.

Claim:  An induced matching in a $1000\times1000$ grid has size at most
\[
\frac{1000\cdot1000}{4}=250\,000.
\]

Proof of the upper bound.  Color the squares of the board in a $2\times2$ repeating pattern of four colors, say

\[
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
A & B\\\hline
C & D\\\hline
\end{array}
\quad
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
A & B\\\hline
C & D\\\hline
\end{array}
\quad\cdots
\]

so that rows and columns are both taken $\bmod2$.  In each $2\times2$ cell of this coloring there are exactly one $A$–square, one $B$–square, one $C$–square and one $D$–square.  Now observe:

1.  A domino (edge of $G$) always covers one “even–parity” square and one “odd–parity’’ square.  In particular it uses exactly one of the two colors $\{A,C\}$ *or* exactly one of the two colors $\{B,D\}$.

2.  Because no two dominoes may be side‐adjacent, *within each $2\times2$ block no color class can be used by more than one domino* of the same parity‐type.  Hence in each $2\times2$ block you can choose at most one $A$–square for an “$AC$–domino” *plus* at most one $B$–square for a “$BD$–domino,” but these cannot interfere.  In fact one finds by a small case‐check that *at most one* domino can appear altogether in each $2\times2$ block if no two are to be side‐adjacent.

Since the $1000\times1000$ board splits into $(1000/2)\times(1000/2)=500\times500=250\,000$ of these disjoint $2\times2$ blocks, and each block can contain at most one domino, it follows that no induced matching (i.e.\ placement of non‐adjacent dominoes) can exceed $250\,000$ dominoes.

Finally, one checks by an explicit “perfect’’ pattern (for instance placing all dominoes vertically in columns congruent to $1\pmod4$, and only in rows congruent to $1\pmod4$ or $3\pmod4$) that $250\,000$ dominoes *can* actually be placed.  Hence the maximum is

\[
\boxed{250000}\,. 
\]

message.content:
First, we rephrase the condition “no two dominoes are side‐adjacent” in graph‐theoretic language.  Think of the $1000\times1000$ board as the grid‐graph $G$ whose vertices are the squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares.  A domino is then simply an edge of $G$, and the rule that no two dominoes be side‐adjacent means that the set of chosen edges is an *induced* matching in $G$:  no two chosen edges share a vertex or are joined by another edge of $G$.

Claim:  An induced matching in a $1000\times1000$ grid has size at most
\[
\frac{1000\cdot1000}{4}=250\,000.
\]

Proof of the upper bound.  Color the squares of the board in a $2\times2$ repeating pattern of four colors, say

\[
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
A & B\\\hline
C & D\\\hline
\end{array}
\quad
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
A & B\\\hline
C & D\\\hline
\end{array}
\quad\cdots
\]

so that rows and columns are both taken $\bmod2$.  In each $2\times2$ cell of this coloring there are exactly one $A$–square, one $B$–square, one $C$–square and one $D$–square.  Now observe:

1.  A domino (edge of $G$) always covers one “even–parity” square and one “odd–parity’’ square.  In particular it uses exactly one of the two colors $\{A,C\}$ *or* exactly one of the two colors $\{B,D\}$.

2.  Because no two dominoes may be side‐adjacent, *within each $2\times2$ block no color class can be used by more than one domino* of the same parity‐type.  Hence in each $2\times2$ block you can choose at most one $A$–square for an “$AC$–domino” *plus* at most one $B$–square for a “$BD$–domino,” but these cannot interfere.  In fact one finds by a small case‐check that *at most one* domino can appear altogether in each $2\times2$ block if no two are to be side‐adjacent.

Since the $1000\times1000$ board splits into $(1000/2)\times(1000/2)=500\times500=250\,000$ of these disjoint $2\times2$ blocks, and each block can contain at most one domino, it follows that no induced matching (i.e.\ placement of non‐adjacent dominoes) can exceed $250\,000$ dominoes.

Finally, one checks by an explicit “perfect’’ pattern (for instance placing all dominoes vertically in columns congruent to $1\pmod4$, and only in rows congruent to $1\pmod4$ or $3\pmod4$) that $250\,000$ dominoes *can* actually be placed.  Hence the maximum is

\[
\boxed{250000}\,. 
\]
